narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 71.7% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the university prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed in their legal careers. For context, the model looks at patterns from thousands of past students, including their grades, test scores, and family backgrounds, to make these forecasts. In your case, as a Black male applicant, the two biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st—meaning your grades placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile—and your Year 1 decile also at 1st, again lower than the average student's position around the middle of their class. These early and later academic struggles stood out as the strongest signals to the model that bar exam success might be challenging, reflecting how consistently low relative performance in school often correlates with exam difficulties. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, while below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor here, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing by showing some resilience compared to even weaker GPAs. Your LSAT score of 29, much lower than the typical 37, and middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Full-time enrollment status, common for over 90% of students, didn't sway things much either. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having academics that fall short in key relative measures, which the model weighs heavily for bar passage risks. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your potential despite these hurdles. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on, and reapplying after targeted improvements could shift your prediction dramatically. You're clearly motivated to pursue law—use this feedback as a roadmap to build a stronger case next time.
